schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch frame chapters 23-26 as one survey of the Levites in David's fortieth year; Ellicott hears 'satisfied with days' (Gen 35:29; Job 42:17) and notes the Chronicler repeats only the bare accession from 1 Kings 1.
Matthew Henry: the crown entailed by divine appointment — Solomon to reign after David, not with him — settled in a solemn assembly for the good of the temple.
Gill sets David near seventy, old and full of days though not yet bedridden.
Clarke lays out the chapter's plan and refers 'full of days' to Genesis 25:8.

themes: David old and full of days

schools.summary: Keil: the princes convened because numbering the Levites was national business; Ellicott dates the assembly to David's close (fortieth year, 1 Chr 26:31) and calls princes, priests, and Levites the 'three estates.'
Gill: the assembly summoned both to name Solomon successor and to settle the temple's service.
Wesley: gathered to declare God's mind and David's will that Solomon succeed, per directions received by the spirit.

themes: the three estates of the realm

schools.summary: Keil harmonizes the ages (Num 4:3 thirty-fifty; Num 8 twenty-five-fifty; v.24 from twenty) and judges David did not confine the count to thirty; Ellicott glosses 'by their polls' as excluding women and children.
Clarke: the fifty-year limit is dropped because, the ark being fixed, there are no more burdens; Wesley: the old limit served the carrying of the tabernacle, a reason now ceased.
Gill: numbered from thirty as in Moses' day, with a new reckoning to follow at v.24.
Ellicott and Keil take vv.4-5 as David's own spoken decree; Ellicott reads 'set forward' as 'superintend' and 'officers' (shoterim) as 'writers' (Exod 5:6; Deut 16:18).
Gill: the twenty-four thousand prepared the sacrifices for the priests, a thousand serving weekly.
Wesley: the officers oversaw the temple work by courses; the judges served not in the temple but through the kingdom in the administration of justice.

Ellicott: the clause 'instruments I made' proves David's spoken decree and marks him an inventor of stringed instruments, hearing Amos 6:5 as honouring him; Keil keeps to the fourfold structure of the division.
Gill: four thousand porters kept the gates in their turns.
Clarke: David had no positive divine authority for the arrangement, and the instruments are condemned in the parallel of Amos 6:5.

debates.question: Had David's institution of temple music and instruments divine warrant?

sides.position: a legitimate, divinely gifted institution — David the inventor of instruments, honoured even in the allusion of Amos 6:5
a pious arrangement of David's own, without positive divine authority, the instruments censured in Amos 6:5

schools.summary: Ellicott: the 'them' divided are the twenty-four thousand superintendents (v.4), sorted 'according to' Gershon, Kohath, and Merari (1 Chr 6:1); the roster's names recur in chapters 24 and 26 but differ from the singers' and warders' lists; Keil weighs the verb's disputed pointing.
Gill: David sorted the Levites into three classes answering to Levi's three sons.

Ellicott: vv.7-11 give nine Gershonite houses, twenty-four courses like the musicians (25:31) and priests (24:4); Laadan a branch prominent in David's day, hardly Libni; Keil expounds how tribes branched into families and fathers'-houses, not always evenly.
Gill identifies Laadan and Shimei with Gershon's sons Libni and Shimhi, heads of the two families.

debates.question: Is Laadan the same as the Libni of the older lists?

sides.position: yes — Laadan is Gershon's son Libni under another name
no — Laadan is hardly Libni, but a later branch prominent in David's day

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: Laadan's descendants named in two groups (this verse and v.9); the 'Shimei' of v.9 a descendant of Laadan, not the brother-Shimei of v.7 whose sons come at v.10 — six houses in all to Laadan.
Gill: these are not Laadan's immediate sons but descendants, the 'chief' a head of house.
Ellicott renders 'heads of the fathers'-houses to Laadan,' the names at once clans and living chiefs; six houses reckoned to Laadan in all.
Gill: this Shimei a descendant of Laadan (with Kimchi), not the son of Gershon.

themes: clan and chief in one name

schools.summary: Ellicott: Shimei's four sons reckoned as three houses (Jeush and Beriah combined); 'Zina' here is 'Zizah' next verse, probably the right form (LXX, Vulg.); Keil sums nine Gershonite houses, six of Laadan and three of Shimei.
Gill: this Shimei is Gershon's son and Laadan's brother (with Kimchi).
Ellicott: Jeush and Beriah, few in sons, became one house and class; nine Gershonite clans in all among the Levites of v.4, and the spelling settles as 'Zizah.'
Clarke: the small family joined a larger so the two together might do the work too heavy for either alone.
Gill: Jahath the chief, prince and head of a family.

themes: the weak joined to the strong

schools.summary: Ellicott: vv.12-20 give nine Kohathite houses (1 Chr 6:1); Keil: the four sons founded the families of Num 3:27, and from Amram came Aaron and Moses, lifting the house above its brethren.
Gill: Kohath the second son of Levi.

themes: the house of priesthood and lawgiver

schools.summary: Ellicott: Aaron set aside for chapter 24, 'hallowed as most holy' (an epithet applied to Aaron nowhere else in Scripture); the words echo Deut 10:8 and Num 6:23; Keil rejects the older glosses about the holy of holies and the sacred vessels.
Clarke: 'to bless in his name' is blessing the people by invoking the LORD; Wesley: 'sanctify' is keeping the most holy things from pollution, the offices named being common to all the priests.
Gill: Aaron and Moses the sons of Amram, a son of Kohath.
Henry: Moses' posterity level with the common Levites shows his self-denial; Aaron's elevation was a recompense for his earlier meekness — those content to stoop are in the fairest way to rise.
Ellicott: Moses' sons 'named of the tribe of Levi' = reckoned common Levites (cf. Gen 48:6); 'man of God' a title of divine mission, from Deut 33:1 to Timothy (1 Tim 6:11).
Wesley: their reduction to common Levites is recorded for the honour of Moses, who left the rule to Joshua and the priesthood to Aaron.
Gill: Moses the man of God — prophet, lawgiver, and guide of Israel through the wilderness.
Henry: Moses sought no dignity for his house — proof he had the spirit of God, not of the world.

Keil: Gershom and Eliezer each found a house (through Shebuel and Rehabiah), the plural 'sons' implying unnamed others; Ellicott parses the names — Gershom 'expulsion,' Eliezer 'God is help,' distinct from Eleazar.
Gill names the two sons of Moses, referring to the earlier record of their birth.
Ellicott: 'Shebuel the chief' implies other unnamed sons of Gershom, reckoned in the one house; Shubael in 24:20; Keil takes him the head constituting the house.
Gill: Shebuel, called Shubael, whom the Targum makes Jonathan.
Clarke relays the Targum's Jonathan legend.

outliers.note: Relays the Targum's legend that Shebuel was Jonathan the false prophet, who repented in old age and was made David's chief treasurer.

themes: one head, a house numbered in him

schools.summary: Ellicott: Rehabiah the sole son and eponym, whose clan 'multiplied exceedingly' (1 Chr 22:5); Moses' descendants comprised in two houses only.
Clarke gives the Targum's 'sixty myriads,' multiplied 'on account of the merits of Moses.'
Gill: Rehabiah the first and only-begotten of Eliezer.
Henry: Rehabiah's increase a recompense for Moses' refusal to be made a great nation.
Ellicott: Izhar made one clan, Shelomith (Shelomoth in 24:22), the same name-variation as v.9; Keil: one son named as head of the Izharite house, the rest reckoned under him.
Gill: Izhar another son of Kohath, taken up after the digression through Amram.
Ellicott: 'of' is wanting in the Hebrew (as vv.16,18,20); Hebron's four houses recur in 24:23; Keil: with Uzziel's two, nine Kohathite houses besides the priests.
Gill: Hebron a third son of Kohath.

Ellicott: Uzziel's two houses (Michah and Jesiah) are rehearsed again in 24:20-25; Keil: with Hebron's four they complete the nine Kohathite fathers'-houses, the priests of Aaron drawn off and Moses' sons numbered as Levites.

Ellicott anchors Mahli and Mushi in the older lists (Exod 6:19; Num 3:33; 1 Chr 6:19); Keil: Mahli's line runs, through Eleazar's heiress daughters married to Kish's sons (Num 36), into the single house of Kish (head Jerahmeel, 24:29).

themes: absorption into the house of Kish

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil: Eleazar's sonless house merged into the sons of Kish (Num 36:6), the line of Mahli represented in David's day by Kish (24:29).
Clarke grounds the marriage in the law for the daughters of Zelophehad (Num 27:1; 36:5).
Gill: Eleazar died leaving daughters, none to succeed in his office and service.

Ellicott: the three sons of Mushi with Kish make only four houses; six are needed for the total of twenty-four, so he strikes the opening 'Mahli' as a scribal repeat of v.21. Keil: rather the Jaaziah material of 24:26-27 is an interpolation, and this chapter's list is sound.
Gill notes only that Mushi was Merari's other son, passing over the difficulty.

debates.question: How are the Merarite houses reconciled to six, for the total of twenty-four courses?

sides.position: an ancient scribe doubled 'Mahli' here; strike it and six clans remain — Jaaziah's houses in chapter 24 being original
the Jaaziah material of 24:26-27 is an interpolation; this chapter's list is itself sound

themes: the count that will not add up
a doubled name or an interpolation

schools.summary: Ellicott: the subscription 'from twenty' clashes with 'thirty' of v.3 — a textual slip, two divergent sources, or (with the older commentators) two numberings; twenty was the military age (Num 1:3); Keil confirms the wording of the subscription.

Henry: David numbered them from twenty by God's direction, confirmed by his last words — because the young were idle till twenty-five, the burden of carriage was past, and the multiplied people needed more hands.
Clarke: a different ordinance from v.3, David's last orders; Wesley harmonizes both figures — from twenty for training, from thirty for full service.
Gill: this is the new reckoning David ordered.

debates.question: How is the numbering 'from twenty' (v.24) reconciled with 'from thirty' (v.3)?

sides.position: two successive numberings or ordinances by David, the age lowered as the work grew lighter
'thirty' in v.3 is an ancient error of transcription
the Chronicler incorporated two divergent statements from his sources

schools.summary: Ellicott: the rest means no more wandering or foreign oppression, and 'He hath settled in Jerusalem for ever' — Zion now God's eternal dwelling (Ps 132:13; cf. 1 Chr 17:5); Keil joins the people's rest and the Levites' release as one gift.
Gill: the reason David gave for ordering the new numbering.
Ellicott: the Levites need no longer carry the dwelling as in the desert; the mishkan long fixed at Gibeon, the old charge including guard against marauders — so a younger man may now serve.
Gill: the rest God gave reaches the Levites too.
Ellicott: 'by the last words of David' = his last commands, or a record of them (cf. 1 Chr 29:29); a parenthetic remark of the Chronicler, breaking David's speech (resumed at v.28).
Gill: David's last orders, given before his death.
Ellicott: the Levites 'stationed at the side of' the priests, over the courts and chambers (1 Chr 9:26) and the purifying of holy things (2 Chr 30:19), marking a permanent institution.
Clarke: the purifying = keeping the vessels clean and neat; Wesley widens 'holy things' to places, garments, vessels, and sacrifices.
Gill: their office was to wait on the sons of Aaron, the priests.
Henry: an honourable drudgery — keeping the courts clean and all things ready to the priests' hand.

Ellicott: 'over the showbread' etc., 'baked in the pan' (Lev 2:5), 'soaked in oil' (Lev 7:12); the Levites kept the standard measuring-vessels (Exod 29:40); Keil: every preparation short of the strictly priestly acts.
Clarke and Wesley: the standards of all weights and measures were kept at the sanctuary and tried by the Levites (Exod 30:13).
Gill: they prepared the showbread weekly for the priests to set out fresh.
Ellicott: the standing to praise belongs to the four thousand musicians (v.5; 1 Chr 16:4), a body distinct from those who slew the victims.
Clarke and Wesley: at the morning and evening sacrifice they sounded instruments and sang, the two solemn times attended with public prayer and thanksgiving.
Gill: others of the Levites sang praises, vocally and with instruments, at the sacrifice.
Ellicott and Keil: 'over all offering' — the Levites select, procure, slaughter, and skin the victims, the priests offering them on the altar, all 'by number' per the law (Num 28) and 'continually.'
Gill: the offering itself was the priests' work; the Levites sang and helped in the slaying.

Ellicott and Keil sum the charge under three heads — tabernacle, holy place, and the sons of Aaron — grounded on Num 18:3; Ellicott defends the Chronicler (against Reuss) from confounding tabernacle and temple, and cites Ewald for the section's antiquity.
Gill: keeping the tabernacle means letting in no unclean person and letting out no vessel — the porters' work.
Clarke: the Levites the priests' servants, flaying the victims the few priests could not, engaged in the more servile part of the worship.
